mechanism_narrative: Sustained increases in industrial production signal rising corporate output and earnings, leading to higher equity valuations over short horizons directly through stronger current earnings and positive sentiment.
failure_condition: Rolling 1-year rank IC between INDPRO monthly change and subsequent 5-day SPX return is below 0.03, or the long-short decile spread is not positive and significant (t-stat > 2) over a 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp rise in the unemployment rate above a critical threshold (6.0%) triggers recession fears, reduces consumer spending and corporate earnings expectations, and pushes equity prices down over the subsequent 20 trading days.
failure_condition: Forward 20-day SPX returns are positive in more than 50% of months following an UNRATE level above 6.0%, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous decline in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) and retail sales (RSAFS) over three consecutive months indicates a broad-based demand contraction that depresses corporate profits and triggers negative equity returns over the next quarter.
candidate_trigger: both PAYEMS and RSAFS decline month-over-month for 3 months
failure_condition: When PAYEMS and RSAFS both decline for 3 months, the subsequent 60-day SPX return is positive more than 40% of the time, or the mean return is not significantly negative (one-sided test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: The impact of a change in the federal funds rate on equity returns depends on the inflation environment: when CPI inflation (CPIAUCSL YoY) exceeds 3%, a rising FEDFUNDS tightens financial conditions and depresses stock valuations, producing negative forward returns over the next month.
failure_condition: In months with CPI YoY > 3% and rising FEDFUNDS, the average 20-day SPX return is positive, or the rank IC of the FEDFUNDS change conditioned on high CPI is not negative at the 5% significance level.

mechanism_narrative: Quarterly growth in real GDP (GDPC1) signals the underlying strength of the economy; higher GDP growth leads to higher corporate earnings and a rising S&P 500 over the following quarter as investors update forward expectations.
failure_condition: The rank IC between quarterly GDP growth and subsequent 60-day SPX returns is below 0.03 over a 10-year rolling window, or the strategy long GDP-up / short GDP-down does not produce a positive Sharpe ratio over any 5-year period.

mechanism_narrative: A drop in housing starts (HOUST) below 1 million units signals a severe housing market downturn, which historically leads to broader economic weakness and negative equity returns over the next quarter.
failure_condition: After HOUST drops below 1000, the average 60-day SPX return is positive, or positive returns occur in more than half of such episodes.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous upward drift in both payroll employment and unemployment rate is a classic stagflation signal: rising employment (higher costs) and rising unemployment (lower demand) squeeze corporate margins and lead to negative equity returns over the short term.
candidate_trigger: both PAYEMS and UNRATE increase month-over-month for 2 consecutive months
failure_condition: When both PAYEMS and UNRATE rise for two months, the subsequent 5-day SPX return is positive more than 45% of the time, or the average return is not significantly negative.

mechanism_narrative: The effect of industrial production growth on equity returns is regime-dependent: when CPI inflation is high (CPIAUCSL YoY > 3%), rapid production growth risks overheating and policy tightening, making further output expansion a negative signal for stocks over the coming month.
failure_condition: In months with CPI YoY > 3% and rising INDPRO, the average 20-day SPX return is non-negative, or the rank IC of the interaction term is not significantly negative at the 5% level.
